ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Japanese curry rice combo set meal; closest_parent 'curry dish' (FOODON_00002809) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Japanese curry rice set meal; A0CKP (rice-based dish) used as closest_parent. The dish is a composite of rice, curry sauce, and accompaniments.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Japanese curry rice combo set meal.

serving.description: 1 set meal (curry rice with half-boiled egg and miso soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice (~200 g) topped with Japanese curry sauce and meat (~250 g), one half-boiled egg (~50 g), and a small bowl of miso soup (~50 g).

nutrient_source.title: Japanese Curry Rice (Kare Raisu) — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked white rice, Japanese-style curry sauce with beef, and hard-boiled egg; cross-referenced with NCC Food and Nutrient Database generic curry rice category
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) and NCC Food and Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a Japanese curry rice combo set meal as served in Singapore. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), beef curry sauce (estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef stew with vegetables and curry spices), and hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424). Miso soup contribution is minor and included in the composite. The per-100g values represent a weighted blend of the full set meal (~550 g total). Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed rice-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese curry rice combo as a complete set meal. GI of 65 is estimated using the GI of boiled white rice (GI ~64–72, medium category) as the dominant glycemic contributor (~200 g of ~550 g total serving), moderated slightly downward by the fat and protein content of the curry sauce and egg, which slow gastric emptying. The University of Sydney GI Database reports boiled white rice GI values in the range 64–72 (medium). A composite GI of 65 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (65 × 89.1 g available carbohydrate per 550 g serving) / 100 = 57.9, rounded to 57.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curry sauce, miso soup, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A single serving of Japanese curry rice combo provides an estimated 1705 mg sodium (~74% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association), primarily from the curry roux, miso soup, and soy-based seasonings; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation or limit portion size to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 65 (medium) and a glycemic load of approximately 58 per full set meal serving, this dish can cause a moderate postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion to 100–150 g, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, and monitoring blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from curry roux and miso soup
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The curry roux contributes saturated fat (~7.7 g per serving) and the combined sodium load from curry sauce and miso soup is high; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this meal to occasional consumption and choose leaner protein options such as chicken or tofu in place of beef to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Japanese Curry Rice Combo (カレーライスセット, Karē Raisu Setto) is a popular Japanese set meal consisting of steamed white rice served alongside or topped with a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce containing meat (typically beef or chicken) and vegetables such as potato, carrot, and onion. The set is accompanied by a half-boiled egg and a small bowl of miso soup, or alternatively a drink. Japanese curry (kare raisu) differs from South Asian curries in that it uses a roux-based sauce that is sweeter, milder, and thicker, with a characteristic umami depth from soy sauce and fruit-based sweeteners. The texture is hearty and comforting, with the curry sauce coating the rice grains. This dish is widely served in Japanese casual dining chains across Singapore, including at Sukiya and similar establishments. Nutritionally, a full set meal (~550 g) provides approximately 649 kcal, 28.6 g protein, 20.9 g fat, and 89.1 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (~1705 mg per serving) due to the curry roux and miso soup, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is approximately 65 (medium), driven primarily by the white rice component, and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 58, which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat content (~7.7 g per serving) is moderate and relevant for cardiovascular disease management. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten, soy, egg, or dairy allergies without modification.

Is curry rice good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a Japanese curry rice combo set meal as served in Singapore casual dining restaurants. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked white rice, beef curry sauce, and hard-boiled egg, weighted by estimated serving proportions. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice as the dominant glycemic contributor. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries (cooked white rice FDC 168878, hard-boiled egg FDC 173424, beef curry sauce estimated from SR Legacy beef stew with curry spices). No HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry was found for this specific dish. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese curry rice set meal portions as described in published Japanese food composition literature; actual restaurant portions may vary. 3. The miso soup component (~50 g) contributes meaningfully to sodium but minimally to macronutrients; its sodium contribution (~300 mg) is included in the composite estimate. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not individually reported by a single source for this composite dish; these values carry higher uncertainty. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; Sukiya Singapore serves beef and the halal certification status of the specific restaurant should be verified independently. 6. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON include 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' and 'Fried Chicken', which appear to be platform-level tags from the restaurant's broader menu rather than descriptors of this specific dish; these tags have not been carried forward into the Veritas record. 7. GI source match type is analogous_estimate; a direct GI measurement for Japanese curry rice combo as a complete set meal was not found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Removed menu-style numbering '966.' from canonical food_name. Added Japanese local name カレーライスセット to satisfy G6 multilingual requirement for Japanese cuisine. Constructed composite nutrient estimates from USDA FoodData Central component data with full provenance documentation. Set GI to 65 (medium) using University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for boiled white rice. Populated all health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Set all -1 sentinel values for micronutrients estimated from USDA components with documentation in notes. Confirmed all ontology match_type values use only allowed strings.

Iteration 2: Verified zero-macro justification rule not triggered (no core macro is zero). Verified GI > 0 rule satisfied (GI = 65). Verified all -1 values are documented in notes. Verified aliases consistency check: all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases. Confirmed final_decision PASS with score 8/8.
